On this day, it's a visit to Palmer Park in Roseland, a far South Side Chicago neighborhood plagued with gang violence.
This is where Emanuel announced he's been able to put more police on the streets, by shifting them from jobs on the inside.
There's nothing more important than the beat officer as the backbone of the police department.
So we have gone through, line by line, to get to a total of around 750 officers who are now in our communities, our neighborhoods, on our blocks throughout our city.
It's too early to determine how successful the mayor is or will be, says former City Clerk Miguel Del Valle, while eating at a neighborhood restaurant.
Del Valle ran for mayor against Emanuel, and says he's reserving judgment.
But even he thinks Emanuel is moving in the right direction.
I like the fact that the mayor keeps telling us every time some corporation decides to establish additional jobs in the city of Chicago.
But I want to make sure that some of those jobs are going to the folks in the neighborhoods of the city of Chicago.
Cheryl Corley, NPR News, Chicago.

You're listening to MORNING EDITION, from NPR News.
It's special coverage, from NPR News.
I'm Steve Inskeep.
And I'm Renee Montagne.
Osama bin Laden was killed yesterday in the Pakistani city of Abbottabad.
Instead of living in a cave in the tribal region of Pakistan, he was living in a large, walled compound.
Joining us now from Abbottabad is NPR's Julie McCarthy.
Good morning.
Good morning.
Tell us about the city.
I know you've just - more or less just arrived there.
Well, actually, Abbottabad is quite a peaceful, beautiful place.
It's an old hill town that was founded by the British back in the colonial area - in the area.
And they pride themselves on their peace.
In fact, Renee, I'm standing, and I am looking at the compound.
The compound is about - maybe 300 yards from where I am standing.
And there is a red - sort of material that has cordoned off the compound that was the scene of this extraordinary operation last night and the - that terrified, basically, this area.
The houses are large.
They have large plots here.
It's kind of like a wealthy suburb of Abbottabad.

And people who allowed me, actually, into their house, snuck me into their bathroom so I could stand up and see a gander - get a gander of the compound because the military has cordoned this off, and they're not allowing the media anywhere near this place.

But they spoke about being absolutely terrified by the helicopters - flew very low - and then, of course, the explosions that followed when the helicopter crashed and then was actually blown up by the Americans in this raid.
And Julie, this is a city where there's a military facility, also a military compound.
What does that suggest about his presence there?
Well, it suggests that either the Pakistanis may have been aware of something, may have tipped off - originally provided some sort of tip to the Americans, or sat on things or was ignorant of it.
It could be a combination of all the - or D, none of the above - or E, all of the above.
And I think, you know, Renee, if you make the decision, gee, maybe we should hide in plain sight where no one would ever expect us to be, you just might come to Abbottabad.
Well, let me get a general...
Well, you know, I got that sense, that valedictory sense a little bit.
But I don't want to overplay it, because I think it also is a strategic move, because as you heard, it got a tremendously strong and positive response from the audience, Alex.

And so while all of us are wondering is there a moment at which she's going to concede or signal that she's giving up, in fact it had a very positive response with a specific demographic that she's interested in, which is making sure she's doing well with American white suburban women, and with the Latino audience.
A lot of people don't like to see the fighting, and so what you saw was that she in fact was extending a hand of grace.
And some people may say, you know what, that's the kind of mature leadership we're seeking.
Well, that may be, but an awful lot of people are already writing about Obama versus McCain, and Senator McCain is talking as though he's running against Senator Obama.
He sure is, Alex.

In fact, after the results came in on Tuesday night, there was Senator McCain saying that Senator Obama is guilty of eloquent but empty calls for change, suggesting that he would have America take a holiday from history and that he's not prepared because of lack of experience, not prepared to deal with world threats.

In fact, Senator Clinton picked up on much of that theme last night, brining in events from Cuba and Serbia, and suggesting again that Senator Obama just doesn't have the wherewithal in terms of experience and sense of maturity to deal with world crises.
But it was McCain's attack on Senator Obama, it seemed to me, that signaled the start of a general election theme here.
The thing is, this line hasn't really worked for her.
You wonder if it's going to work any better for him.
You know, I was talking to some Republicans about this because it occurred to me that, just as you just said, it hasn't worked for her.
And of course Senator Obama picked up on this line of thought, saying you're making it out like all of my supporters have been duped or fooled or don't know who I am and don't know my background.

But the Republicans, Alex, think this, that John McCain, someone who served in the military, someone who's a little bit older, someone who has always been strong on military issues and expressed a great deal of concern about the U.S.

military actions in Iraq, they think Americans are going to be more tuned in in a general election in which Republicans focus on Republicans as better defenders of the nation than Democrats historically, and that that contrast will have some power in the general election that it has not had in the primaries.
There's been some focus this week on a story the New York Times published yesterday about Senator McCain, alleging that years ago he may have had inappropriate contact with a lobbyist.
Here is part of Senator McCain's very firm denial of that story.
At no time have I ever done anything that would betray the public trust, nor make a decision which in any way would not be in the public interest and would favor anyone or any organization.
Juan, you have read, as I have, a lot of criticism of the New York Times for publishing this story.
But I have one question about Senator McCain's response to it.

In his statement he says I have never done a favor for a lobbyist, I've never done a favor for a special interest, when in fact he was one of the Keating 5, these senators who did favors for the man who subsequently went to jail for a big part in the savings and loan scandals of the early 90's.
And he was to have exercised - I think poor judgment was the term that was used by a Senate committee that investigated that.
You know, I sat down with Senator McCain for lunch - I guess it was about a year ago, Alex.
And in the course of the conversation, the Keating 5 scandal came up.
And the way that he treats it is, it's the most searing experience of his political life and one that set him on the path to being a reformer.
NPR News analyst Juan Williams in New York.
Good luck getting back home to D.C.
this weekend.
Yeah, I'll get my sled dogs out, Alex.
California will have to change its welfare system to comply with new federal standards, but former welfare mothers like Diana Spatz say this is the wrong way to do it.
I recently spoke with Spatz, who spent 10 years on welfare before earning her bachelor's degree from the University of California Berkeley.
After graduating, she founded Lifetime, a welfare advocacy group based in Oakland.
Spatz credits welfare benefits were giving her second chance at life.
I was homeless while I was pregnant because of domestic violence.
So, literally, it was my safety net.

If it wasn't for welfare, I don't know how I would have been able to get a place to live, get medical care, prenatal care and ultimately, you know, provide at least the basics for food and shelter for me and my daughter.
Is this something that's typical or atypical for people who go on welfare?
Oh, it's very typical.
The California Institute for Mental Health, its studies that show that like as high as 83 percent of mothers on welfare in some California counties are domestic violence victims.
Nationally, the figure studies have showed 65 percent of mothers on welfare are battered women with children.
So my experience is not the exception, it's the rule.
What exactly is the governor, Governor Schwarzenegger, proposing be done to the current system in California?
Basically, they're targeting children.
Mothers who are sanctioned, meaning they get cut off the welfare grant because they have been unable to do a welfare to work activity that their case worker is requiring.
Right now, under our system, just the mother gets taken off the grant.
Under the proposed changes, the whole family would be sanctioned, including the kids.

It would put five year lifetime limits on children's eligibility for welfare, both children whose parents don't get welfare like immigrant parents who aren't eligible and as well as parents who've reached their five-year lifetime limit, those kids would be sanctioned as well.
So basically it's sanction on low-income children, poor kids.

Explain, though, if you've got a family where, say, it's a single-parent family with a mother, and that mother has been on welfare for five years and come up against a time limit or that mother has not met a work requirement and yet the kid is still getting money.
That money goes to the parent, that goes to…
The entire family.
Huh.
We're going to meet him later.
Right.
Very interesting response here.
They basically said: Look, he's dead.
It's a good thing.
It helps eradicate one part of the global terrorism that has been at the root of so much of Pakistan's problems.
America, mission accomplished.
Now, what you need to be thinking about is ending - you and the Western allies - your presence in Afghanistan, and no longer conducting any sort of operations in Pakistan.
As much as people might have wanted to see the demise of Osama bin Laden here, they view what happened here last night, in this raid, as a direct assault on the sovereignty of Pakistan.
It's pretty clear, as the facts are coming out, that this - that the Pakistanis were not involved in this.
